mnemonics.title: Packet Switching Components
mnemonics.content: P.A.C.K.E.T. = Payload + Address + Control + Key (VCI/Sequence) + Error + Trailer

mnemonics.title: Types of Delays
mnemonics.content: D.E.L.A.Y. = Delivery + Encoding + Link + Accumulation + Yours (queuing)

mnemonics.title: Datagram vs Virtual Circuit
mnemonics.content: D.A.T.A.G.R.A.M. = Destination Address Taken - Aims to Reach independently, whereas V.I.R.T.U.A.L. = Virtual Identifier Routes Traffic Uniformly Along Link

acronyms.usage: Used in virtual circuit switching to identify the established connection path
Maximum packet size that can be transmitted without fragmentation (typically 1500 bytes)
Mechanisms to guarantee specific performance characteristics for packet-switched traffic
Error detection field in packet trailer for verifying data integrity
Congestion control algorithm that drops packets before buffers overflow

visualTips.visualization: Draw a rectangle divided into three sections: Header (address info, blue), Payload/Data (main content, green), Trailer/Error control (red) - like a letter with envelope (header), letter content (payload), and seal (trailer)
Imagine multiple roads from city A to B - datagram is like taking different roads for each car (packet), virtual circuit is like a dedicated bus lane where all buses follow the same route
Think of it as relay race - each runner (router) receives the baton (packet), holds it securely, then passes to next runner - ensures baton is not dropped but takes time at each handoff

analogies.analogy: Like postal service - each letter (packet) is sent independently, can take different routes, may arrive out of order, but all get delivered to the destination address.
Like a train reservation - you book a specific route (virtual circuit), all carriages (packets) follow the same track, arrive in sequence, and the path is pre-established.
Like sending a group text - each message goes out independently, recipients can receive in any order, no advance setup needed.
Like waiting at a toll booth during peak hours - packets (cars) must wait their turn even though the road (link) exists, causing variable delays.
Like deliveries from different courier services - some arrive quickly, some get stuck in traffic, causing unpredictable arrival times.
